turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: We'll hear argument next in Number oh one four fifty five, the Franconia Associates versus United States, and the Grass Valley Terrace versus United States. Mr.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: Justice, and may it please the Court.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Mr. Eckland, you you began by saying you're seeking fair compensation for something.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: We are Justice Scalia, we are seeking compensation for the breach of contra-

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: you think you have a separate cause of action for the Government's violation of the Constitution, assuming y- that that that you're correct that that's what it's done?

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: Oh, quite so, Your Honor, yes.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: It is with the Department of Agriculture, Your Honor, through the...

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: Well, it c- it comes no.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER
turns.text: with your argument is that if you have your whole property claim here rests upon the contract.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: Well, petitioners pled, Justice Souter, the takings claim in the alternative to the breach of contract claim.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER
turns.text: If they are correct, then I don't see how you've got a taking because your your whole property interest the only property interest you're asserting is an interest under that contract.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: What's being taken, Justice Souter, is the expected use of the petitioners' properties.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: to a certain period of time.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER
turns.text: contract for it, you have no right to expect it.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: But there were representations by the Government that created a reasonable investment backed expectation on...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: on the part of the pet-

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: did the lower courts find that there was no contract claim? Because of the statute.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: Because of the w- one judge, in particular, because of the unmistakability doctrine.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: the contract was not clear enough to constitute an enforceable contract with the Government.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: I thought the Federal Circuit went on the statute of limitations.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: on the basis of the statute...

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: you presented is whether a breach of contract claim accrues for purposes of section twenty-fi- when Congress enacts a statute alleged to abridge a contractual right to freedom from regulatory cavants [: covenants] upon prepayment of Govern- mortgage loans.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: And that's the case we have here.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Didn't the Federal Circuit rely on anticipatory breach?

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: on anticipatory breach, as I understand it.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: We do not either, Your Honor, although the petitioners here have the option of suing assuming suing before the breach, assuming that they had the ability to perform at that time and there's nothing in the record...

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: the notion that before they went to pay it off, it was an anticipatory breach.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: that the reason the statute of limitations doesn't arise, doesn't start to run prior to the time that they paid it off, is because prior to that time, there was no actual breach.

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: but you take the position that your clients could have filed suit f- soon after the passage of ELIHPA.

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: take the position let's be clear about...

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: please that after the enactment of the statute, your c- clients could have filed suit for breach of contract on an anticipatory breach theory?

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: Yes, but only if they had the ability to prepay at that time.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: when when the obligation to perform comes.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: You do have to show that you're but for the repudiation, you do have to show that you are able to perform.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER
turns.text: about here in performance means w- w- the ability to prepay.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: I I have a contract to deliver a hundred thousand widgets three years from now.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: anticipatory breach most people use it not to get damages but to relieve themselves of the obligation of of remaining i- i- in in the status of being able to perform the contract.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: It's never the case that he's ready to or almost never the case that he's ready here and now to perform.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: But, Your Honor, if the person who had the obligation to accept the the delivery did not have the funds, for example, even to purchase them, then that would be sort of...

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: the funds until three years from now.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER
turns.text: this? Could we agree to this, that so far as the element of an anticipatory breach claim involves the act of the Government, you had that element, and at least to that extent, you could have brought an anticipatory breach claim when the statute was passed? Maybe you didn't have other elements.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER
turns.text: and But you're saying, whether I could've sued then or not, I certainly can sue at the point at which I would claim my right to redeem without these various conditions, and they refused to honor it.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: And and the doctrine does not require you to sue until that date.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: for total control then over timing because your client could say, well, we're not ready after five years, we're not ready after seven years, we're not ready after ten years.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: Because the Government negotiated option terms up to fifty...

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: deal, and the owners relied on that when they signed up, and they had, you know, a clear expectation that at some point in time they would be able to prepay, opt out, and convert their...

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Did they have a a right to assume right after the day after the statute was passed, did they continue to have a right to prepay,

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: N- Well, the right still existed, Your Honor, but it wasn't it was repudiated at the time of the statute.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Well, it wasn't just repudi- it's not like a private citizen saying, I don't intend to perform three years from now.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: maintain there was no breach at the time the law was passed?

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: that the statute changed the -- your rights.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: The statute did condition them, and that conditioning, or fettering as the Government says, is a repudiation, but like any other situation in the private sector, when you repudiate a right, the breach doesn't occur until the time for performance comes due.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well, the breach is repudiation if...

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: to change its mind? Couldn't the Government pass another statute going back to what ze [: the]

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Yes, Justice Scalia, they could and they did.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: seventy-nine they initially conditioned the right, and in nineteen eighty they repealed it, retracted it.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: argu- about is whether a statute itself, without any ac- action from the agency, can be a repudiation.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: We believe it can be a repudiation, yes, Mr. Chief Justice.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: D- Does the repudiation give you the right to the same damages that you would receive for a total breach of the contract?

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: I think it's usually the case, is it not, that when there's an anticipatory repudiation, it's v- it's very difficult for the the other party to determine what his damages are going to be.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: is why he uses the doctrine to get out of the contract rather than to seek damages.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: Petitioners here, being in the Court of Federal Claims, have as their only remedy the monetary judgment.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: So you are you s- you're saying that there was a claim, or you're saying that that your claim really doesn't accrue until you until you have the pre- the wherewithal to prepay.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: We're saying th- the petitioners would at least have to have the money to prepay.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: intent to perform and have the...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: I have a problem with that,

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: c- if we can get b- b- for the same reason Justice Scalia mentioned.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: cash in the bank that would allow you to prepay, and then there's a...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: At that point, can't you show your damages? You don't want to prepay, but can't you say have an expert in and say, this property has a certain value and part of the value is the right t- i- i- at any time to b- prepay the the the loan and and use it for another use? And this can be valued in the real estate market.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: But here, Justice Kennedy, a fifty year option exists.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: fifty year options are not subject to valuation? The experts do this all the time.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: Well, but even so, even if you could come up with a damage theory to cover this, still it's clear that under the law, the breach would not be deemed to accrue for limitations purposes until the time for performance has arrived and performance failed.

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: You're you're just getting me confused again.

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: what in the world you are arguing.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: if they had the ability to pay, you say.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: According to Corbin and the case law that we cite at footnote twenty-three of our brief, yes.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: twenty-three just says that ordinary contract law applies to these this kind of case.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: We still do- but we so we still have to figure out what ordinary contract law, footnote twenty-three doesn't shed any light on that that I saw.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: page twenty-seven of the blue brief you say that the law constituted an anticipatory repudiation and petitioners had the option of suing immediately.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: In general, yes, that's true, Your Honor.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: But the all petitioners commence suit within six years of the date of the actual breach, which is the date that the prepayment requests were denied.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: So, what apparently the Government's arguing does it matter if we call this anticipatory repudiation or anticipatory breach? I guess technically...

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: we should say anticipatory rebudiation? [: repudiation]

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: And so what they say, in part, which I'd like hear your response, is that you're right that where there is anticipatory repudiation of a contract, the other side, if it's a private contract, has a choice.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: Well, here Your Honor, although, yes, we need to construe the statute strictly, we cannot do so in such a manner, however, to narrow the waiver of sovereign immunity that has already occurred under the Tucker Act.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: then i- if that's the ca- I me- I mean I really do think that your takings claim, w- w- which is the reason you're going into this thing well, it was just a conditioning of the right it wasn't an elimination of the right it just reduced it you you do all of that to to support your takings claim.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: how you can say it was enough for an anticipatory breach, but it wasn't enough for a taking.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: It it you are correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: And that that's why you were confusing Justice O'Connor,

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: not really ready to say there was an anticipatory breach, because in order s- to sustain your takings claim, you don't really want to say there was an anticipatory breach because they didn't really, absolutely break the contract.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: both shoulders it seems to me, and and and you're spilling a lot.

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: in every breach there's also a taking?

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: Why aren't weren't you satisfied with a breach of contract claim?

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: what are you adding with this so called takings claim?

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: Well, in the event that the lower courts do not rule in the petitioners' favor on the contract claim, they have their wholly independent takings claim, which is based not it's not predicated on the existence of a contract.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Suppose you win on the first claim.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Suppose you win on the contract claim.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: Well, there are differences between the claims, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: full benefit of your contract, which is what would happen if you win on your contract's claim.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: If well in practical terms, Justice Scalia, if we do prevail on the contract claim at trial, I don't see that we would pursue the takings.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: you're asking us to do then is to write a little essay on a matter that affects millions of other people in a very serious way, in a case which doesn't seem completely to present the issue.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: On behalf of petitioners I mean, we would not...

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: to dismiss it too, I suppose.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: May I ask, because I I have to confess, I get getting a little confused, too.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: All we got before us the takings claim.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: Prior to the time that the owners entered into this program, they owned their land.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: Then they voluntarily agreed to these restrictions on the use, only a certain level of rent, only a certain income of tenants.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Well, well would you tell me what you think was taken? Is it the right to prepay that was taken?

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: If there is no contract right, then no, that was not taken, Your Honor.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: their w- is is the is the permitted use of the property under their reasonable invex- investment backed expectation.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: It's a regulatory taking of the right to make use of the property the way you wanted.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: Which occurred prior to the time that they voluntarily agreed to participate in the program.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: You mean before even before they signed the contract?

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: at or about the same time.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: assume you never made a contract claim.

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: It's just their inability to go back to using their proper- they the w- the way they were because of the passage of ELIHPA and the regulations that compelled them to continue charging only a certain level of rent.

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: back a little? Did your clients borrow some money from the Federal Government at low interest rates?

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: The construction loan, Your Honor, in many cases was in- was initially from a private mortgage lender.

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: the borrower, your clients, had to exers- execute a loan agreement a and a- a promissory note...

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: And was the right of prepayment spelled out in any of those agreements?

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: The prepayment right itself was in the promissory note, yes,

turns.name: JEFF H. ECKLAND
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice I see that most of my time is spent.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Mr. Roberts we'll hear from you.

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: [NS] Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court.

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: do you concede or should we take this case on the assumption that there was a contract? Do do we...

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: assume it for purposes of deciding this case?

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: For the purposes of deciding the statute of limitations question, yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: the contract included a right of...

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: Included a right of prepayment and not only a right of prepayment, because if the contract just said you can prepay at any time, it like all contracts would be presumed to be subject to subsequent legislation.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: the my contract to pay a million dollars when my ship when the ships with grain come in, I say, a year in advance, ha ha, I'll never pay.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: cannot cannot break a contract then.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Well, the Government can always act by legislation, can't it?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: and the Government can breach the...

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: contract and and be responsible for damages.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: as it does it by legislation, it's okay.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: thought that was your point, that that this m- this breach is different because it was done by legislation.

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: That doesn't mean that that doesn't mean that it's not a breach, but what it means is that the breach is occurring at the time the legislation is...

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: just curious to get into that a little bit is, I don't understand why the Government is taking the position it does, as well as what the position is.

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: What what we're what we're asking for is that people who believe that their contractual rights have been injured have been infringed by a statute give the Government reasonably prompt notice, in accordance with the statute of limitations, of their claims.

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: The the the damages are that petitioners had a loan that they allege gave them a an unfettered option to prepay at any time.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: to measure? Y- You you bet your life on that? I mean, the the reason the reason contract law has developed the option of suing for anticipatory breach as just an option is precisely because it is often so difficult to anticipate what your damages will be.

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: The damages, first of all, can be measured, as I said.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: mean, you know, my life expectancy can be measured, but I wouldn't bet a whole lot of money on it.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: you can come up with a guess for for for anything.

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: With With due respect, Your Honor, I d- I don't think that's the reason why the law gives the the option to the innocent party.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: if it is an option, why should the par- innocent party be penalized by having the sta- statute of limitations start to run then?

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: It's t- not an option here because the statute is a present breach.

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: to understand the nature of the promise that's that's that's at issue here, I think, and if if I could try to go back to...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: going to ask I I thek [: think] I s- I think it's consistent with w- the Chief Justice inquiry.

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: Our position is that there is an actual breach at the time that the statute is passed, and it's a breach of of the promise that the Government made that it wouldn't impose additional legal restrictions on prepayment.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Even though performance is not yet due.

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: It Performance on that promise is due, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER
turns.text: to any contract that is ever made.

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: No, Your Honor, because s- because the the the difference is between legislation and the and the role that legislation has under background principles of contract law and other...

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER
turns.text: principle of contract law is that we try to treat the Government and a private contracting party together.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER
turns.text: saying the fact that the Government can speak through legislation, whereas a private party cannot, alters that rule.

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: The The distinction Your Honor is that is is is two fold that legislation changes the legal rights and so there isn't an...

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER
turns.text: It may convert one right into a a a right to damages as opposed to a right to performance but the theory of contract is that by repudiation you can't just change the legal rights.

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: It does it does exactly that.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER
turns.text: then exactly the same thing is true in a private contract when there has been an anticipatory...

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: No, no, Your Honor, there might be a right to f- specific performance if it was the kind of contract that you could get specific performance, but we would submit that you couldn't get specific performance once the Government...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: How does the fact that you cannot...

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: what it does is show that there's i- there there's a change and an an injury right at the time that the legislation is...

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: It it's not going to apply in a private context because there isn't going to be a breach by virtue of of legislation unless there's a promise...

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: that the laws aren't going to...

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: and, bingo, there is a breach of contract and you must sue at once.

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: You have no need to invent that.

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: Y- Y- You have no need to to have that additional promise in the private contract because because there isn't the presumption.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well, what is what is your authority...

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: for the proposition that the y- and an anticipatory breach not accepted by the other party starts the statute of limitations rim- running? I mean, what case?

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: W- it's My My My My argument is not that this is an anticipatory breach.

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: that has to be there in a contract with the Government...

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: that it's a present breach? Do you still say that an anticipatory breach starts the statute running?

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: That that's not our principal submission.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: you could read the statue you...

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: could read the statute that w- providing first accrues to mean that the first that when a plaintiff can first bring suit, that that's when the statute of limitations starts to...

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: that's not the law as between private parties.

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: Yes, given the principles of sovereign immunity and the principle that the statute should be narr- narrowly...

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: narrow construction notion, or construed strictly notion, applies to deciding whether there's a waiver by the Government of any...

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: The Court the Court has held several times that statutes of limitations, as conditions on the Government's waiver of its sovereign immunity, should themselves be be narrowly construed and has applied it in in in cases, for instance, involving a situation where a private party claimed that the limitations period didn't run until an administration administrative determination had been made that there wouldn't be that there was a lo- w- was a loss...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: in this sense, the Government actually needs less protection than the private party because the Government at least is in a position where it can always pass a statute of limitations...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: and if you're if you're if you're concerned about your liability fifty years out or something, I suppose you could pass a statute of limitations.

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: The Government ha- can do that and has done it here, and the statute of limitations provides that when the action first accrues, there's six years to sue.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, but ag- again, I'm I'm back to the problem.

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: In the in the ordinary situation, if a private party let's let's i- if if I may, let's c- can I pose a hypothetical? of a b- If if a if a private party had promised that you could prepay, that the other party could prepay at any time, and Congress passed a statute that imposed restrictions on prepayment, that statute would be neither an anticipatory repudiation or a breach, but would provide a discharge.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: a- about on the merits of the plantiff's...

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: you're saying that the in Government contracts, there is a promise and there is an issue whether the Government means that promise irrespective of what Congress does in the...

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: what the Government meant was irrespective of what Congress does, and you're saying no, it meant only if Congress doesn't do to the contrary.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: merits, on the merits, yes. But But...

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: that kind of a contract issue is breached when Congress passes the law to the con-

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: if it's just a background principle, I would worry about the practical consequence being of people, particularly in real estate contracts, having to study every regulation, every statute in order to tell their clients what to do.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: And it's also important that Congress be able to cause a closi-

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: It could v- enact the the Congress that's close to the first one could say, okay, we'll repeal it.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER
turns.text: Mr. Roberts, in the case in which the Government, we'll assume, doesn't change its mind, your argument in in response to Justice Breyer's question seems to boil down to something like this.

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, W- When when there's a present when there's a breach, the the general principle is the statute of limitations starts to run at the time of the breach.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: It doesn't wait to run until...

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER
turns.text: your argument, as I understand it, is that because of the Government's peculiar power to pass legislation, the Government should not be in the position of the usual contract breaker who may be subject to an anticipatory breach claim or an actual breach claim later.

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: se- sus- there's a a very strong interest in permitting the Government to in permitting Congress to decide it wants to wrap up the costs of improvident Federal...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: contracts that they have and and t-

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER
turns.text: why don't we let Congress survey through the departments of the Government how many contracts it has entered into or guaranteed and taught up the damages in advance? The Government has access to this information if it wants to get it.

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: wi- The th- the Government doesn't know who's going to sue, for one.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER
turns.text: the Government may get off a little cheaper if it puts the burden on the...

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: i- there's a dispute here as to whether the contract made this promise or not, and the Government doesn't doesn't know how that dispute's going to be resolved.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: contract law is not appried [: applied] you know, you know, you could say it from the point of view of general contract law, it's important that people who breach contracts know as soon as possible how much damage they've done, but obviously that doctrine has not commended itself in the area of general contract law.

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, when they breach a contract and it's a breach, the the statute of limits does start to run and the rule is that even if the damages can't be fully ascertained, that that the statute of limitations run.

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: it's so hard to look at this as an actual breach if we take it on the assumption there was a contractual right of prepayment on demand by the borrower.

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: Even under the scenario you posed that that there wasn't an incentive to prepay right at the moment, there's still a a change in what i- in in in the loan that petitioners have, and it's a loan where they no longer have that option to prepay...

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: the legislation, there still is consideration of the possibility of prepayment, but there're some new conditions imposed whereby the Government tries to assure itself that there will still be a certain number of low-income housing units out there on the market.

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: ye- ye- ye- Yes, Your Honor, but petitioners here aren't complaining about the fact that they're ultimately not able to prepay.

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: there was a request to prepay.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: all petitioners have made requests to prepay.

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: some of the petitioners in this case.

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: to prepay and it's been refused.

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: At that point, there's an exacerbation in our view of the of the previous breach because it's an application of the restrictions that were imposed and that were imposed, according to petitioners' allegations, in violation of the Government's promise not to impose them.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: declaratory relief to determine your liabilities? Pick out...

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: I suppose that the Government could could do that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: for sure, but I don't know any...

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: and you may not know the answer.

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: you would y- y- i- it's I Do I have something that's that...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: case that you were bringing up.

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: I don't think that the c- that the case I I don't have a case one way or the other...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: I think you'll find it's an anticipatory breach.

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: If you didn't make any pro- any other promise at all, it would be an anticipatory breach.

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: But you wouldn't have need to make that other promise because the because it would be presumed that you wouldn't take action that would d- d- you wouldn't be excused by taking action that makes it impossible for you to perform.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: And your whole case hines [: hinges] on that, that the...

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: rule for it would that make you...

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: All I can say to to that, Your Honor, is that that there are many situations in which damages cannot be ascertained fully.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: and in not having to wait for fifty years to know...

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: should want to have some rules out there that would encourage it to be able to deal with people on a commercial basis in some areas.

turns.name: MATTHEW D. ROBERTS
turns.text: I I don't think it discourages them, Your Honor, because they they can sue and their damages are measurable, and they can get the damages.
